content: Hello — I'm CosmoLab AI, your regulatory and quality transformation advisor. I'm here to build a clear picture of your current compliance posture and identify where the highest-risk gaps are. Let's start with scope: which markets do you currently manufacture for, and which product types — cosmetics, OTC drug products, dietary supplements, or a mix?

We manufacture for the US primarily — about 85% of our volume. We have some EU business through a brand that distributes in Germany and France, probably 10% of volume. The remaining 5% is Canada. Product types: about 65% cosmetics, 25% OTC drug products (primarily acne and sunscreen), and 10% supplements.

That's a significant regulatory footprint — you're operating under US FDA cosmetics (MoCRA), US FDA OTC drug cGMP (21 CFR Parts 210/211), EU Cosmetics Regulation 1223/2009, DSHEA, and Health Canada simultaneously. Let me start with MoCRA since it's the most recent and often the least fully implemented. How far along is your MoCRA compliance program — specifically: safety substantiation status for your cosmetic portfolio, responsible person designation, and product listing in the FDA CARES portal?

MoCRA is partially done. We have a responsible person designation in place. Product listing — we've listed about 60% of our cosmetic portfolio; the other 40% is behind because our regulatory team is small (2 people) and they've been prioritizing the OTC work. Safety substantiation is the biggest gap — we have safety data for most products but it's not compiled in a format that would constitute a formal substantiation dossier. It's in various places across product files.

A 40% product listing gap is a real exposure — MoCRA listing is legally required for cosmetics marketed in the US and FDA enforcement is ramping up. The safety substantiation gap is the more significant medium-term risk: while FDA hasn't issued broad enforcement actions yet, the requirement is live and the expectation is that manufacturers have substantiation on file and accessible.

On OTC drug products — you mentioned acne and sunscreen. Those are both FDA monograph products. How would you characterize your cGMP compliance posture for the OTC drug lines? And when did you last have an FDA inspection, and what were the findings?

Our cGMP posture is solid for the core requirements — batch records, specifications, testing. Last FDA inspection was 18 months ago, OTC lines only. We received two 483 observations: one on our CAPA system (investigator noted that CAPAs weren't being formally closed out in our QMS) and one on laboratory data integrity (some raw lab data entries lacked contemporaneous dating — a documentation practice issue, not an analytical error). We submitted a response and it was accepted. We haven't had a follow-up inspection.

Two 483 observations, both accepted — that's a manageable history. But the CAPA observation is a repeat risk if your QMS CAPA closure is still running outside the system. Is that gap resolved since the inspection?

And on the lab data integrity observation — was that on your in-house lab or the contract lab, and has the documentation practice been formally corrected with a procedure update and training record?

The CAPA gap is partially addressed — we've been better about closing CAPAs in MasterControl for new investigations, but there's still a backlog of older CAPAs that were never formally closed. On the lab data integrity: it was in-house. We updated the SOP and did training. We have the training records. I'm reasonably confident that specific issue is resolved.

The open CAPA backlog is a risk — if FDA returns and finds CAPAs that were open at the prior inspection still unresolved in the system, that's a repeat observation, which carries more weight than a first-time finding. I'd prioritize closing that backlog before any next inspection.

Let me move to EU. You said you have EU volume in Germany and France. Do you have a formal EU Responsible Person designated, your products notified in CPNP, and Article 10 Product Information Files with safety assessments completed?

We work through a European distributor who acts as the responsible person — they handle CPNP notifications for the products they distribute. Article 10 safety assessments — this is where I'm less confident. The distributor has told us they have safety assessments, but we've never reviewed them and they're not in our files. We're essentially trusting that the distributor is compliant.

That's a significant compliance gap. As the manufacturer, you have joint liability with the responsible person for product safety. If a product is recalled or investigated by a German or French market authority, 'our distributor said they had it covered' is not a defensible position. You need to request and retain copies of the Article 10 safety assessments and the CPNP notification confirmations.

A few more questions. How does your team currently track regulatory changes — MoCRA updates, EU regulation amendments, new restricted ingredient decisions? And how did you first learn about MoCRA?

We learned about MoCRA from a trade association newsletter — probably 6 months after it was signed into law. Our regulatory intelligence is mostly reactive: newsletters, conferences, occasionally a regulatory consultant we bring in. We don't have a formal process for monitoring changes or assessing impact on our portfolio.

Learning about a landmark piece of legislation 6 months post-signing through a newsletter is a reactive posture that creates risk at your scale — particularly with EU regulations which change more frequently. For a CM with your regulatory scope, a more systematic approach (regulatory intelligence software or a retained regulatory consultant on a monitoring retainer) is a reasonable investment.

Last area: supplier qualification. How do you manage your approved supplier list, CoA review, and incoming QC for raw materials?

We have an approved supplier list in a spreadsheet. CoA review is done by QC when materials are received — we check that results are within spec. Incoming QC testing: we do skip-lot testing for most materials, full lot testing for OTC drug actives. We had one significant supplier issue two years ago — a silicone supplier sent a mislabeled batch and it made it to the manufacturing floor before someone caught the discrepancy. That was a wake-up call but we haven't done a formal supplier qualification program overhaul since then.

A mislabeled batch reaching the manufacturing floor is exactly the scenario that supplier qualification programs are designed to prevent. Skip-lot testing relies on supplier CoA accuracy — if a supplier mislabels, skip-lot provides no protection. For OTC drug actives, FDA expects identity testing on every lot, not skip-lot.

I have everything I need. Type 'generate' to create your regulatory transformation documents.
